catalog_card.shortCardDescription: An 8-hour private car charter built around your ship's all-aboard time, not a fixed schedule. Gangway pickup at Incheon Cruise Terminal, English-speaking driver-guide, and a customizable route through Gyeongbokgung Palace, Bukchon Hanok Village + Insadong, lunch of your choice, N Seoul Tower, and Myeongdong or Jogyesa Temple. The driver-guide holds a 60-minute traffic buffer before sailaway so you never miss the ship.

itineraryStops.name: Cruise terminal gangway pickup
itineraryStops.description:
Private gangway pickup at Incheon Cruise Terminal — your English-speaking driver-guide meets you at the terminal entrance with a name placard, and your private vehicle (sedan or SUV) is reserved exclusively for your group with no other passengers. 

After clearing Korean immigration (~30 min budget), the day's start time is FLEXIBLE around your specific ship arrival/disembarkation timing — communicate ship details to the operator at booking, and the driver-guide adjusts the entire 8-hour window accordingly, including a guaranteed return buffer before your ship's all-aboard time. 

The 60-90 min drive to central Seoul travels via the Yeongjong Bridge and Incheon-Seoul Expressway. Beyond the cruise terminal itself, the Songdo International Business District (opened 2003 as Korea's first Free Economic Zone) is a fully-planned 'smart city' with **Songdo Central Park** (a saltwater canal park 5 minutes by car) as a viable weather-fallback short-detour option for guests whose Seoul time gets compressed by delayed disembarkation. 

The private charter format gives the driver-guide flexibility to add or skip Songdo Central Park based on real-time gangway timing. **As of January 1, 2026**, South Korea requires a **mandatory digital e-Arrival Card** for visitors without K-ETA exemption — completing K-ETA registration before sailing (USD 8, 3-year validity) eliminates the form-fill step at the gangway. 

The terminal is **7 km from the nearest Incheon Subway Line 1 station** ('International Business District Station') — not walkable, so private charter eliminates the only practical alternative (₩10,000 taxi to metro + ₩90-minute Subway Line 1 + Seoul Subway transfer).
highlights: Private gangway pickup at Incheon Cruise Terminal
English-speaking driver-guide; private sedan or SUV (your group only)
60-90 min drive to central Seoul via Yeongjong Bridge + Incheon-Seoul Expressway
Flexible start time built around your ship's specific arrival/disembarkation
**South Korea's largest cruise terminal** — 2-story 7,364 m² building (1F arrivals / 2F departures); USD 580M total project investment; opened **April 25, 2019**
**Berths Royal Caribbean Oasis-Class** ships (228,000 GT, 6,370 passengers + 2,400 crew) — also handles MSC Bellissima, Diamond Princess, Spectrum of the Seas (2026/27 season)
**Private gangway pickup**: driver-guide commits to your group only — no shared-passenger waits at gangway, departure timed to your group's disembarkation pace
**200-vehicle parking lot** including 156 large-bus capacity; private charter typically uses sedan / minivan / SUV slots near terminal exit
**Mandatory digital e-Arrival Card since January 1, 2026** for visitors without K-ETA exemption — recommend K-ETA registration before sailing (USD 8, 3-year validity)
timeUsed: Disembark cruise ship and clear Korean immigration (~30 min)
Meet driver-guide at terminal curbside with name placard
Begin private 60-90 min drive to first Seoul stop
Pre-departure check: K-ETA / e-Arrival Card status verification (~5 min)
Optional Songdo Central Park weather-fallback detour (~15 min if applicable)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Private gangway pickup is the day's foundational guarantee — the driver-guide is committed to your group only, the ship all-aboard time is built into the schedule, and the language-barrier challenge of taxis and public transit is eliminated. For cruise guests prioritizing flexibility over cost, private charter eliminates the shared-van waiting window at the gangway, the fixed-departure pickup time, and the inflexibility of itinerary changes mid-day. The driver-guide can add or remove stops based on real-time interest (e.g. swap Myeongdong for Hongdae if your group leans young-traveler) and the all-aboard time is built into a 90-minute reboarding buffer.

visitBasics.hours: Cruise-call dependent; pickup is after immigration clearance at the terminal.
visitBasics.closed: Available only when the cruise ship calls at Incheon.
visitBasics.admission: No attraction admission; terminal access follows port and cruise security rules.
visitBasics.walking: Easy - short walk from gangway/arrival hall to curbside pickup.

convenience.parking: Tour bus and taxi stands outside the terminal; driver-guide meets at the assigned curbside point.

smartNotes.tip: Provide ship name + scheduled arrival + all-aboard time at booking. The driver-guide will arrive 30-60 min after your scheduled docking to allow immigration clearance. Cruise terminal Korean address: 인천항 크루즈터미널 (연수구 국제항만대로 438).

itineraryStops.name: Gyeongbokgung Palace + Royal Guard ceremony
itineraryStops.description:
Gyeongbokgung — 'Palace Greatly Blessed by Heaven' — was the main royal palace of the Joseon Dynasty (1392-1910), built 1395 in the 3rd year of King Taejo's reign as the heart of the new capital Hanyang. It is the largest of the Five Grand Palaces and once contained 330+ buildings serviced by 3,000 staff. 

Burned to the ground during the 1592 Imjin War, the palace lay in ruins for 273 years until King Gojong rebuilt it in 1867; the Japanese colonial administration demolished much of the rebuilt complex 1910-1945, and restoration begun in 1990 continues today. Three centerpieces define the visit: **Geunjeongjeon Throne Hall** — the largest wooden building in Korea, where kings held court and received foreign envoys; **Gyeonghoeru Pavilion** — a two-story banquet pavilion on 48 stone pillars over a lotus pond; and **Hyangwonjeong Pavilion** — Korea's most photographed garden scene, a hexagonal pavilion on a small island reached by a covered bridge. 

The **Royal Guard Changing Ceremony** at Heungnyemun Gate (10:00 + 14:00 daily, ~20 min) recreates the Joseon-era palace gate-protection ritual — guards in vivid blue, red, and yellow uniforms with traditional drums and horns. Your driver-guide times arrival for the 10:00 ceremony when feasible. 

**Tuesday note**: Gyeongbokgung is CLOSED ON TUESDAYS — when this falls, the tour substitutes Changdeokgung Palace (UNESCO World Heritage 1997, closed Mondays instead) — the only palace listed by UNESCO for its 'outstanding example of Far Eastern palace architecture and garden design integrated into and harmonized with the natural setting.'
highlights: Joseon Dynasty's main palace — built 1395 as the heart of new Seoul
Geunjeongjeon Throne Hall — largest wooden building in Korea
Gyeonghoeru Pavilion — 48-pillar banquet pavilion on lotus pond
Hyangwonjeong Pavilion — Korea's most photographed garden scene
Royal Guard Changing Ceremony at Heungnyemun Gate (10:00 + 14:00 daily)
**Closed Tuesdays** — substitute Changdeokgung Palace (UNESCO World Heritage 1997)
FREE admission for hanbok-wearers

**Royal Guard Changing Ceremony re-enacted since 1996** based on Cultural Heritage Administration historical research — went on hiatus after 2008 Sungnyemun arson incident, resumed 2016 (with the 70+ performer authentic-armor format used today)

**3 additional ceremonies between main 10:00 + 14:00 changes**: Gwanghwamun Gate Guard Ceremony at 11:00 + 13:00; Public Training of Royal Guard Soldiers at 09:35 + 13:35 — total 6 daily reenactments (except Tuesdays)
timeUsed: Driver-guide drop-off at Gwanghwamun Square (~5 min)
Royal Guard Changing Ceremony if 10:00 timing (~20 min)
Throne Halls + Gyeonghoeru + Hyangwonjeong walk (~35-40 min)
Photo time + reassembly at vehicle
Optional National Folk Museum or National Palace Museum extension (within grounds, free) (~15 min if interested)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Gyeongbokgung is Seoul's #1 must-see and the natural opener for a private cruise excursion. The flexible private-tour pace allows lingering at Royal Guard ceremony and pavilion photography that group bus tours rush. For cruise guests on private charter, the 90-min Gyeongbokgung dwell adapts to your group's pace — historians can extend at the throne hall while photo-takers move ahead to Gyeonghoeru Pavilion, with the driver-guide flexing the schedule by ±15 min. Picked over Deoksugung (Western-influenced, smaller) because the changing-of-the-guard pageantry, scale of the throne-hall complex, and photogenic Gyeonghoeru/Hyangwonjeong pavilions deliver a single 90-min visit that explains 500 years of Joseon court life in concrete visual terms — essential for cruise passengers with one day in Korea. Tuesday-arrival groups get redirected to Changdeokgung (UNESCO World Heritage 1997).

visitBasics.hours: Mar-May, Sep-Oct: 09:00-18:00; Jun-Aug: 09:00-18:30; Nov-Feb: 09:00-17:00 (last admission 1 hr before close)
visitBasics.admission: Adult 3,000 KRW / Child 1,500 KRW; FREE for hanbok-wearers
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — flat extensive grounds; ~1.5-2 km of walking through main complex

convenience.restroom: At main entrance, mid-grounds, rear pavilion area
convenience.parking: Driver-guide drops at Gwanghwamun and parks separately

smartNotes.photo: Gwanghwamun gate at sunrise / golden hour. Royal Guard Changing Ceremony 10:00 + 14:00. Gyeonghoeru reflected in lotus pond. Hyangwonjeong covered bridge in spring (cherry blossoms) or autumn (foliage).
smartNotes.facilities: National Folk Museum + National Palace Museum INSIDE Gyeongbokgung grounds (free admission). Cafes, restrooms, gift shop near entrance.
smartNotes.tip: Time arrival for 10:00 Changing of the Guard. Skip Tuesday — Changdeokgung substitute. If your group is interested in palace architecture beyond surface-level photos, ask the driver-guide for a 30-min walking commentary on Joseon hierarchy and royal etiquette.

itineraryStops.name: Bukchon Hanok Village + Insadong
itineraryStops.description:
Bukchon ('Northern Village') is a 600-year-old hanok (traditional Korean tile-roofed house) residential district nestled between Gyeongbokgung and Changdeokgung Palaces — historically home to high-ranking Joseon Dynasty officials and aristocrats — and today Seoul's largest concentration of preserved hanok homes (~900 traditional houses). 

The signature route walks the **Bukchon 8 Gyeong (8 Famous Views)** with the iconic #6 viewpoint at Gye-dong-gil framing cascading hanok rooftops with N Seoul Tower in the distance — Seoul's most-photographed traditional architecture scene. Bukchon is a LIVING village (not a museum) — residents have publicly requested respectful behavior including quiet hours and limited photography of doorways. 

From Bukchon's southern edge, **Insadong** (인사동) — Seoul's premier traditional crafts and cultural street — is a 5-10 minute walk. Insadong combines the 4-story spiral **Ssamzigil** complex (70+ craft shops, galleries, cafes, rooftop terrace), traditional teahouses (Mokyeon, Suyeonsanbang), hanji (mulberry paper) workshops, ceramic galleries showing Goryeo celadon and Joseon white porcelain, and Korean souvenir vendors. 

Pedestrian-only on weekends (Sat-Sun). Your driver-guide's flexibility lets you spend more time wherever the group's interest pulls — Bukchon photography vs. Insadong shopping is a natural in-the-moment choice. 

The village's preservation is overseen by **Jongno-gu District Office's Bukchon Hanok Village preservation program** — strict zoning rules limit commercial signage, renovation must use traditional materials (handmade hanji paper screens, wooden frames, tile roofs), and the Red Zone entry restriction from 17:00 to 10:00 is enforced by signage and community wardens during peak season.
highlights: Bukchon 8 Gyeong (8 Famous Views) — iconic hanok-roofline + N Seoul Tower at #6
~900 preserved hanok homes — Seoul's largest traditional residential district
Insadong Ssamzigil 4-story spiral cultural complex (70+ shops + rooftop terrace)
Traditional teahouses, hanji paper workshops, Goryeo celadon galleries
5-10 min walk between Bukchon and Insadong
Pedestrian-only weekends (Sat-Sun)
**~600 years old** — historic district between Gyeongbokgung and Changdeokgung Palaces, originally home to Joseon Dynasty high-ranking officials and aristocracy

**Red Zone entry restriction 17:00–10:00 enforced** — village is a LIVING residential community (not a museum); cultural respect protocol critical (no flash photography in alleys, voices down, no drones)
**Combination Royal Palaces Ticket ₩10,000 adult / ₩5,000 child** covers all 4 royal palaces + Jongmyo Shrine — 3-month validity; savings vs single tickets if pairing with private hanok-tour day
timeUsed: Driver-guide drop-off near Bukchon entrance (~5 min)
Bukchon 8 Views walk + photos (~30-40 min)
Walk south to Insadong (~5-10 min)
Insadong Ssamzigil + craft shopping (~30 min)
Reassembly at vehicle
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Pairing Bukchon and Insadong in a single 75-min stop reflects the private-tour flexibility — your driver-guide drops at Bukchon's north end and picks up at Insadong's south end, eliminating backtracking. Picked over the Namsangol Hanok Village curated open-air museum because Bukchon's living-village authenticity plus Insadong's craft-shopping commercial appeal cover both 'traditional residential' and 'traditional crafts' narratives in one walk. The private-charter format means the driver-guide can drop at Bukchon's north end (Bukchongil Road) and pick up at the south end near Insadong, eliminating backtracking and saving ~10 min vs the bus-tour drop-and-return pattern.

visitBasics.hours: Bukchon 24/7 (Red Zone restricts tourist entry 17:00-10:00; quiet conduct requested throughout residential lanes); Insadong shops 10:00-22:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (some Insadong galleries closed Mondays)
visitBasics.admission: Free; pay-as-you-go for purchases, teahouses, workshops
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — Bukchon has cobbled, sloped alleys; Insadong is flat

convenience.restroom: Tourist Information Center at Bukchon entrance + Anguk Station Exit 6

smartNotes.photo: Bukchon 8 Gyeong #6 viewpoint at Gye-dong-gil. Hanbok-wearers framed against tile-roof gables. Ssamzigil spiral interior + rooftop terrace. Korean wedding-color threads at hanbok-accessory shops.
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist Information Centers, traditional teahouses, hanji workshops, ceramic galleries, multiple cafes throughout. Balwoo Gongyang (Michelin temple cuisine) at Jogyesa nearby.
smartNotes.tip: Visit Bukchon before noon when residents are out + lanes are quietest. Insadong best on weekends when pedestrian-only. Best souvenirs: hanji paper crafts, traditional ink stamps (dojang), small ceramics, traditional tea sampler boxes. If interested in hanbok rental — shops cluster at Bukchon's southwestern edge, ~15,000-20,000 KRW for 4 hr.

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (your choice)
itineraryStops.description:
Lunch is YOUR choice and timed flexibly around the day's pace — your driver-guide offers three vetted options matched to your group's preferences: **(1) Gwangjang Market food alley** — the working-class streetfood experience opened 1905 (Korea's first permanent traditional market), made world-famous by Anthony Bourdain's 'Parts Unknown.' 

Bindaetteok mung-bean pancakes (5,000-7,000 KRW), mayak gimbap, yukhoe raw beef tartare. CASH-ONLY at most stalls. **(2) Insadong traditional restaurant** — sit-down Korean cuisine in a hanok setting; hansik prix-fixe sets featuring banchan (side dishes), bibimbap, kimchi-jjigae stew. 

Mid-tier 15,000-25,000 KRW per person. **(3) Myeongdong Kyoja or Hadongkwan** — Korean institutions: Myeongdong Kyoja for handmade kalguksu (knife-cut noodles) and mandu since 1966; Hadongkwan for Seolleongtang (oxbone soup) since the 1930s. Mid-tier 12,000-20,000 KRW. 

**(4) Royal court cuisine at Korea House** — premium hanjeongsik (table d'hôte royal cuisine) in a hanok complex (recognized with three ribbons in the 2025 Blue Ribbon Survey); 60,000-120,000 KRW per person, requires 60-min commitment. Communicate dietary needs (vegetarian, halal, no spicy) at booking — driver-guide adjusts accordingly.
highlights: Lunch venue YOUR choice — 4 vetted options (street food, traditional, institution, royal court)
Gwangjang Market: working-class streetfood paradise (Bourdain-famous)
Insadong hanok prix-fixe: mid-tier sit-down Korean (~15,000-25,000 KRW)
Myeongdong Kyoja / Hadongkwan: Korean dining institutions
Korea House: royal court cuisine premium experience (60,000-120,000 KRW)
Dietary needs accommodated at booking
timeUsed: Driver-guide drop-off at chosen venue
Meal (~45-50 min)
Reassembly at vehicle
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Genuine lunch customization is the private-tour value driver — group bus tours commit to a single lunch venue, but private cruise passengers often have specific dietary needs or strong preferences (e.g., 'no streetfood' for some / 'authentic only' for others). The 4-option menu covers 95%+ of mainstream cruise-passenger preferences without forcing a single choice.

visitBasics.admission: Pay-as-you-go (12,000 — 120,000 KRW range depending on choice)

smartNotes.tip: Communicate dietary preferences (vegetarian, halal, no-spicy, gluten-free) at booking. Royal court cuisine at Korea House requires advance reservation — book 24+ hr ahead. Cash recommended for Gwangjang Market option (10,000-20,000 KRW per person).

itineraryStops.name: N Seoul Tower (Namsan)
itineraryStops.description:
N Seoul Tower (Namsan Seoul Tower) is Seoul's most iconic landmark — a 236.7-meter broadcasting and observation tower atop Namsan Mountain (243 m), with the tower's apex 479.7 m above sea level. Originally constructed 1969-1971 as Korea's first comprehensive broadcasting tower (TV/radio multi-broadcaster), it opened to the public 1980 and was rebranded 'N Seoul Tower' in December 2005 after CJ Foodville renovated the observation levels. 

Recognized by foreign tourists in a 2012 Seoul Metropolitan Government survey as Korea's #1 tourist attraction, and selected with Changdeokgung as one of Lonely Planet's Ultimate Travel List Top 500 destinations (2015) — approximately 12 million annual visitors. 

The signature visitor experiences: **the 360° observation deck** (Adult 21,000 KRW / Senior+Child 16,000 KRW; weekdays 10:30-22:00, weekends 10:00-23:00 — last admission 30 min before close); the iconic **Locks of Love wall** on the terrace where couples attach padlocks; the revolving **N Grill restaurant** (one rotation per ~48 min for a panoramic dining experience — diners with advance reservation get FREE observatory access); and **Korea's first aerial tramway** the **Namsan Cable Car** (opened 1962, predates the tower by 9 years, 605 m route, ~3 min ride, max altitude 138 m). 

K-drama filming legacy includes 'My Love From The Star,' 'Boys Over Flowers,' 'Korean Odyssey (Hwayugi).' **Critical note**: TAXIS ARE PROHIBITED from entering the park (environmental protection) — your private vehicle drops at the Namsan Cable Car base or Namsan Yejang parking, and you ride the cable car or walk the final ~10 min.
highlights: Seoul's most iconic landmark — 236.7 m tower atop Namsan (479.7 m above sea level)
Constructed 1969-1971, opened 1980, rebranded N Seoul Tower 2005
#1 tourist attraction by foreign-tourist survey (2012); 12 million annual visitors
360° observation deck (Adult 21,000 / Senior+Child 16,000 KRW)
Iconic Locks of Love wall + N Grill revolving restaurant (free observatory with reservation)
Namsan Cable Car — Korea's FIRST aerial tramway (1962), ~3 min ride
K-drama filming: My Love From The Star, Boys Over Flowers, Korean Odyssey
**Taxis PROHIBITED from entering the park** — cable car or shuttle bus only
**Namsan Cable Car (1962)** = Korea's FIRST aerial tramway; pre-N-Seoul-Tower-rebrand the structure was simply called 'Seoul Tower' from 1980 to December 2005 (CJ Foodville renovation + rebrand)

Cable car ride up (~3 min) OR shuttle bus (~5 min)

itineraryStops.name: Myeongdong OR Jogyesa Temple (your choice)
itineraryStops.description:
The day's final substantive stop is YOUR CHOICE between two distinct Seoul finales: **OPTION A — Myeongdong**: Seoul's #1 shopping and K-beauty district in Jung-gu. Pedestrian-friendly zone with restricted vehicle access. Major Korean cosmetics flagships (Innisfree, Etude House, Laneige, Olive Young, Missha) with generous free samples. 

Myeongdong Cathedral (built 1898 — Korea's first brick Gothic Catholic church, played significant role in democratization movement). Lotte Department Store flagship (1970s, multi-floor luxury including Avenuel designer wing). Street food: tornado potato skewers, hotteok pancakes, egg bread, cheesy corn dogs, tanghulu sugar-glazed fruit skewers. 

CASH-ONLY at most street stalls (bring 20,000 KRW). Open daily; shops 10:30-22:00, street food 12:00-22:00 (later on weekends). **OPTION B — Jogyesa Temple**: Head temple of the Jogye Order — Korea's largest Buddhist Order. Origins back to 1395 (Gakhwangsa); modern temple founded 1910; renamed Jogyesa in 1954 after the Buddhist Purification Movement. 

Daeungjeon (Main Dharma Hall) is the largest temple building in Seoul. Natural Monument No. 9: 500-year-old Baeksong (Lacebark/White Pine, 14 m tall, 1.85 m trunk). 450-year-old Chinese Scholar Tree. 

Ten-story stone pagoda housing 10 Buddha sari gifted by Thai royal court. FREE admission. Annual Lotus Lantern Festival around Buddha's Birthday (May) is UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage — temple courtyard fills with thousands of paper lanterns. 

The only major traditional temple in Seoul's city center (most Korean temples are in mountains). 5-min walk to Insadong. Your driver-guide takes you to whichever option fits your group's interests — Myeongdong for shoppers/foodies; Jogyesa for cultural/spiritual depth.

itineraryStops.name: Return to Incheon Cruise Terminal

Private return drive **60-90 minutes** to **Incheon Cruise Terminal** — your driver-guide adjusts the day's pacing throughout to ensure arrival **90+ minutes before your specific ship's all-aboard time**.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A point-to-point loop that starts and ends at your ship's gangway. The Seoul itinerary itself is a cluster, not a line — Gyeongbokgung, Bukchon, Insadong, and Myeongdong are all within a 20-minute radius of each other, which is what makes a 7–9 hour port day workable. The driver-guide's main job, after the cultural commentary, is keeping your sail-away time visible at every transition.

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Built around your all-aboard time, not a fixed clock
items.detail: Standard group tours run on the operator's schedule, not yours. A private cruise excursion starts the moment you disembark and ends well before your all-aboard, with a 60-minute safety buffer that absorbs Seoul traffic. The driver-guide treats the ship's departure time as the only deadline that matters.

items.label: Pickup at the gangway, not 'meet at a station in Seoul'
items.detail: Cruise terminals at Incheon are 50 km from central Seoul. Public transit means a taxi to Incheon Airport, then the AREX train, then a subway line, then walking — easily 90+ minutes each way and a real risk if delayed. A private car is direct, door-to-door, and your luggage stays in the vehicle if you want.

items.label: No mystery shopping stops, no group of 40
items.detail: Many large cruise-line excursions include forced ginseng-shop or duty-free stops that eat 60–90 minutes of your day. A private car has no such commercial pressure: you stop where you want, for as long as you want, in your own group only.

items.label: Customizable on the day
items.detail: Royal guard ceremony cancelled by rain? Skip Bukchon, add Jogyesa? Want to swap Myeongdong for the Korean War Memorial because your father served? All possible — a private guide-driver can rebuild the day mid-itinerary as long as the sailaway window holds.

staticQuestions.question: What if my ship is delayed arriving at Incheon?
staticQuestions.answer: The driver-guide tracks the cruise schedule and adjusts pickup time accordingly. If the delay is significant (over 2 hours), the day's itinerary may need to be shortened — your guide will recommend the best subset to fit the remaining window.

staticQuestions.question: What if Gyeongbokgung is closed?
staticQuestions.answer: Gyeongbokgung is closed on Tuesdays. If your port day falls on Tuesday, the substitute is Changdeokgung Palace and its UNESCO-listed Secret Garden (Huwon) — equally worth the visit, sometimes preferred by repeat visitors.

staticQuestions.question: Can we go to the DMZ instead?
staticQuestions.answer: Generally no — DMZ tours from Incheon are tight on time (DMZ is ≈ 50 km north of Seoul, the cruise terminal is ≈ 50 km west). For most cruise schedules, a DMZ + Seoul highlights combo doesn't fit. If your port call is 10+ hours and you want DMZ-only, ask in advance — a separate Incheon-DMZ private tour is offered.

staticQuestions.question: How does the guide know our ship's schedule?
staticQuestions.answer: You provide the ship name, port-call date, expected arrival time, and all-aboard time at booking. The guide cross-checks against the published cruise itinerary and the Incheon Port Authority's daily schedule. Any change communicated by your cruise line should also be relayed to the guide via WhatsApp.

staticQuestions.question: What if there's heavy rain or a typhoon?
staticQuestions.answer: The tour runs in light rain. In a typhoon or heavy snow, the itinerary shifts indoors (palace covered halls, Jogyesa, Myeongdong's underground arcade, museum stops). The driver carries umbrellas. Outright cancellation only if a typhoon also cancels your ship's port call.

staticQuestions.question: Can we leave luggage in the car?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — many cruise guests use the day to also handle a hotel transfer or duty-free errand with luggage on board. The vehicle is locked when not in use; valuables should still be carried with you. Luggage capacity depends on vehicle size, so advise volume in advance.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Seoul?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Moderate walking with some active sections.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for families whose kids can handle a full day of moving around.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors; less ideal for low-mobility travelers.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is around age 6 and up — children comfortable with a full-day rhythm and the walking notes above.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A point-to-point loop that starts and ends at your ship's gangway. The Seoul itinerary itself is a cluster, not a line — Gyeongbokgung, Bukchon, Insadong, and Myeongdong are all within a 20-minute radius of each other, which is what makes a 7–9 hour port day workable. The driver-guide's main job, after the cultural commentary, is keeping your sail-away time visible at every transition.

lessIdealFor: Travelers wanting a fixed-group tour with set departures (this is private; book individually)
Cruise groups larger than the vehicle's seating (typical sedan 1–3 pax, SUV 4–6, van 7+; specify at booking)
Budget-first travelers — private format costs more than shared cruise excursions
Travelers whose all-aboard window is shorter than 7 hours (hard fit)

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Built around your all-aboard time, not a fixed clock
items.detail: Standard group tours run on the operator's schedule, not yours. A private cruise excursion starts the moment you disembark and ends well before your all-aboard, with a 60-minute safety buffer that absorbs Seoul traffic. The driver-guide treats the ship's departure time as the only deadline that matters.

items.label: Pickup at the gangway, not 'meet at a station in Seoul'
items.detail: Cruise terminals at Incheon are 50 km from central Seoul. Public transit means a taxi to Incheon Airport, then the AREX train, then a subway line, then walking — easily 90+ minutes each way and a real risk if delayed. A private car is direct, door-to-door, and your luggage stays in the vehicle if you want.

items.label: No mystery shopping stops, no group of 40
items.detail: Many large cruise-line excursions include forced ginseng-shop or duty-free stops that eat 60–90 minutes of your day. A private car has no such commercial pressure: you stop where you want, for as long as you want, in your own group only.

items.label: Customizable on the day
items.detail: Royal guard ceremony cancelled by rain? Skip Bukchon, add Jogyesa? Want to swap Myeongdong for the Korean War Memorial because your father served? All possible — a private guide-driver can rebuild the day mid-itinerary as long as the sailaway window holds.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.content: Private vehicle (sedan, van, or large van depending on group size), English-speaking driver-guide, fuel, tolls, parking. Not included: admission tickets (Gyeongbokgung ₩3,000, N Seoul Tower deck ₩21,000, etc. — paid on the day), meals, tips. The guide pre-buys palace tickets to skip lines if requested.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.content: Driver-guide meets you at the Incheon Cruise Terminal arrival hall with a name sign. Note that Incheon has multiple terminals — confirm with your cruise line which one your ship uses (Yeongjong Island airport or the Songdo cruise terminal). Pickup time is calibrated to your disembarkation, not a fixed clock.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Sail-away timing
practicalAccordionItems.content: The driver-guide will ask for your all-aboard time at pickup. The standard rule: you must be back on the ship at least 30 minutes before sail-away (most cruise lines). The driver builds at least a 60-minute traffic buffer on top of that. Seoul-Incheon transfers can take 60–90 minutes depending on rush hour.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Customization & dietary needs
practicalAccordionItems.content: The itinerary is fully customizable. Tell us in advance about: dietary restrictions (halal, vegetarian, kosher, allergies), mobility limitations (wheelchair access at palace and tower), specific sites (Korean War Memorial, Lotte Tower observation deck, Hangang River). The driver-guide will pre-plan the route accordingly.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup. Same-day cancellations and no-shows are non-refundable. If your ship's port call is cancelled by the cruise line, full refund with documentation.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Children & accessibility
practicalAccordionItems.content: Children of all ages are welcome — child seats available on request (no extra cost, but please advise number and age in advance). Accessible vehicles available for wheelchair users — please request when booking, as not all vehicles in the fleet support a wheelchair lift.

practicalWeatherStatic._seasonalSummary: Seoul has four distinct seasons. Cruise calls in spring and autumn are typically the most pleasant; summer is hot and humid; winter is cold and dry but rarely freezing-rain.

_seasonalTemperatures.notes: Cherry blossoms in early–mid April. Best season for outdoor sightseeing.
Hot and humid; July can have monsoon rain. Most palaces and museums are partially shaded.
Best season — clear skies, mild temperatures, foliage in late October.
Cold winds; bring a warm coat for the palace courtyard. Tower views often crystal clear.

seasonalVariations.highlight: Cherry blossoms at Gyeongbokgung and along the Cheonggyecheon Stream; Bukchon's traditional houses in spring greens.
Long daylight hours mean you can squeeze N Seoul Tower into a tight window; many indoor museum options if it's hot.
Maple foliage at Gyeongbokgung's rear garden (Hyangwonjeong) and along Bukchon ridges; clearest air for tower viewpoints.
Snow on the palace courtyards is one of Seoul's classic photographs; fewer crowds at major sites.

staticQuestions.question: What if my ship is delayed arriving at Incheon?
staticQuestions.answer: The driver-guide tracks the cruise schedule and adjusts pickup time accordingly. If the delay is significant (over 2 hours), the day's itinerary may need to be shortened — your guide will recommend the best subset to fit the remaining window.

staticQuestions.question: What if Gyeongbokgung is closed?
staticQuestions.answer: Gyeongbokgung is closed on Tuesdays. If your port day falls on Tuesday, the substitute is Changdeokgung Palace and its UNESCO-listed Secret Garden (Huwon) — equally worth the visit, sometimes preferred by repeat visitors.

staticQuestions.question: Can we go to the DMZ instead?
staticQuestions.answer: Generally no — DMZ tours from Incheon are tight on time (DMZ is ≈ 50 km north of Seoul, the cruise terminal is ≈ 50 km west). For most cruise schedules, a DMZ + Seoul highlights combo doesn't fit. If your port call is 10+ hours and you want DMZ-only, ask in advance — a separate Incheon-DMZ private tour is offered.

staticQuestions.question: How does the guide know our ship's schedule?
staticQuestions.answer: You provide the ship name, port-call date, expected arrival time, and all-aboard time at booking. The guide cross-checks against the published cruise itinerary and the Incheon Port Authority's daily schedule. Any change communicated by your cruise line should also be relayed to the guide via WhatsApp.

staticQuestions.question: What if there's heavy rain or a typhoon?
staticQuestions.answer: The tour runs in light rain. In a typhoon or heavy snow, the itinerary shifts indoors (palace covered halls, Jogyesa, Myeongdong's underground arcade, museum stops). The driver carries umbrellas. Outright cancellation only if a typhoon also cancels your ship's port call.

staticQuestions.question: Can we leave luggage in the car?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — many cruise guests use the day to also handle a hotel transfer or duty-free errand with luggage on board. The vehicle is locked when not in use; valuables should still be carried with you. Luggage capacity depends on vehicle size, so advise volume in advance.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Seoul?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Moderate walking with some active sections.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for families whose kids can handle a full day of moving around.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors; less ideal for low-mobility travelers.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is around age 6 and up — children comfortable with a full-day rhythm and the walking notes above.
